Fig. 10: Performance across the number of evidence clues.

Fig. 1: Example tasks from EC-Bench. The benchmark evaluates two abilities for long-form video understanding: enumeration, which identifies all relevant instances (e.g., listing brands or flavors), and counting, which aggregates occurrences of instances across long temporal contexts. Questions are grounded in sparse evidence spans within videos longer than 30 minutes.
Fig. 3: Task statistics of EC-Bench. Top: reasoning category distribution for Enumeration (left) and Counting (right). Bottom: answer distributions for Enumeration (left) and Counting (right).

Table 2: Performance of open-source and proprietary MLLMs on EC-Bench. Accuracy (%) is reported for six reasoning categories and their overall average for each task. #Fr. denotes the number of input frames used by each model. Best and second-best results
Fig. 4: Video statistics of EC-Bench. Left: distribution of video durations (all videos exceed 30 minutes; median 47 minutes). Right: representative examples of video genres in the dataset.

Table 1: Comparison of representative datasets for long-video understanding and counting tasks. EC-Bench uniquely supports ultra-long videos, multi-hop reasoning, explicit evidence spans, and open-ended enumeration.
Fig. 15: Prompt for evaluating enumeration-style answers using an LLM-as-Judge.
Fig. 12: QA generation prompt for long-video Enumeration and Counting tasks.
